source_paper_title: Counting and Enumerating Optimum Cut Sets for Hypergraph k-Partitioning Problems for Fixed k
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Beideman_2024, title={Counting and Enumerating Optimum Cut Sets for Hypergraph k-Partitioning Problems for Fixed k}, volume={49}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2022.0259}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2022.0259}, number={4}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Beideman, Calvin and Chandrasekaran, Karthekeyan and Wang, Weihang}, year={2024}, month=nov, pages={2579–2601} }
context:
Page 24, Section 7 (Conclusion)

Resolving the complexity of this variant of the hypergraph k-partitioning problem for \(k \ge 5\) remains open.
title: Determine complexity of minimizing sum of boundary cut sizes in hypergraph k-partitions
background_latex:
Let \(G=(V,E)\) be a (multi-)hypergraph with vertex set \(V\) of size \(n\) and hyperedge set \(E\). Each hyperedge \(e\in E\) is a subset of \(V\). (The paper primarily discusses the unweighted case, i.e., each hyperedge has unit cost; the objective below is stated for this setting.)

For a nonempty proper subset \(U\subset V\), define the cut-set
\[\delta(U):=\{e\in E: e\cap U\neq\emptyset \text{ and } e\cap (V\setminus U)\neq\emptyset\},\]
and its cut value \(d(U):=|\delta(U)|\).

A \(k\)-partition of \(V\) is an ordered tuple \((V_1,\dots,V_k)\) of pairwise disjoint nonempty subsets whose union is \(V\).

For a \(k\)-partition \((V_1,\dots,V_k)\), the paper considers various objectives based on the boundary sizes \(|\delta(V_i)|\). In particular, the variant highlighted in the conclusion asks to minimize the sum of boundary cut values
\[\sum_{i=1}^k |\delta(V_i)| = \sum_{i=1}^k d(V_i).\]
problem_definition_latex:
Given a hypergraph \(G=(V,E)\) and a fixed integer \(k\ge 5\), determine the computational complexity of the optimization problem
\[\min\left\{\sum_{i=1}^k |\delta(V_i)| : (V_1,\dots,V_k) \text{ is a } k\text{-partition of } V\right\}.\]
In particular, decide whether this problem admits a polynomial-time algorithm for fixed \(k\ge 5\) (or whether it is NP-hard) in the unweighted hypergraph setting.
latex:
Let \(G=(V,E)\) be a (multi-)hypergraph with vertex set \(V\) of size \(n\) and hyperedge set \(E\). Each hyperedge \(e\in E\) is a subset of \(V\). (The paper primarily discusses the unweighted case, i.e., each hyperedge has unit cost; the objective below is stated for this setting.)

For a nonempty proper subset \(U\subset V\), define the cut-set
\[\delta(U):=\{e\in E: e\cap U\neq\emptyset \text{ and } e\cap (V\setminus U)\neq\emptyset\},\]
and its cut value \(d(U):=|\delta(U)|\).

A \(k\)-partition of \(V\) is an ordered tuple \((V_1,\dots,V_k)\) of pairwise disjoint nonempty subsets whose union is \(V\).

For a \(k\)-partition \((V_1,\dots,V_k)\), the paper considers various objectives based on the boundary sizes \(|\delta(V_i)|\). In particular, the variant highlighted in the conclusion asks to minimize the sum of boundary cut values
\[\sum_{i=1}^k |\delta(V_i)| = \sum_{i=1}^k d(V_i).\]

Given a hypergraph \(G=(V,E)\) and a fixed integer \(k\ge 5\), determine the computational complexity of the optimization problem
\[\min\left\{\sum_{i=1}^k |\delta(V_i)| : (V_1,\dots,V_k) \text{ is a } k\text{-partition of } V\right\}.\]
In particular, decide whether this problem admits a polynomial-time algorithm for fixed \(k\ge 5\) (or whether it is NP-hard) in the unweighted hypergraph setting.
open_problem_quote: Resolving the complexity of this variant of the hypergraph k-partitioning problem for \(k \ge 5\) remains open.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: This is a complexity-status question (polynomial-time solvable vs NP-hard) rather than a universal inequality that can be refuted by a finite instance search. While one can experiment with heuristics or reductions, automated counterexample search is not a faithful route to resolving the stated problem.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: An evolutionary coding loop could explore algorithmic ideas (e.g., dynamic programming for fixed \(k\), relaxations, or exact solvers on structured instances) and test performance, but empirical success would not establish polynomial-time solvability or hardness. It may still help by discovering patterns or candidate reductions/algorithms that can later be proved.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The objective in the open problem is the minsum boundary variant of hypergraph \(k\)-partitioning: minimize \(\sum_{i=1}^k d(V_i)\) where \(d(U)=|\delta(U)|\) is the (unweighted) hypergraph cut function. In the notation of the source paper, this is distinct from Hypergraph-\(k\)-Cut (which minimizes \(|\delta(V_1,\dots,V_k)|\) counting each crossing hyperedge once) and from Minmax-Hypergraph-\(k\)-Partition (which minimizes \(\max_i d(V_i)\)). The source paper develops powerful terminal-cut structural theorems (notably Theorem 1.4) enabling deterministic enumeration for Hypergraph-\(k\)-Cut and Minmax-Hypergraph-\(k\)-Partition for fixed \(k\), but it explicitly notes that analogous structure fails for the minsum boundary objective and leaves its complexity for \(k\ge 5\) open.

Subsequent work on submodular partitioning has made partial progress on the minsum side. The strongest direct advance toward the stated objective is the polynomial-time algorithm for minimum 4-partition of a general submodular function, which implies a polynomial-time algorithm for minimum 5-partition when the function is symmetric submodular; since hypergraph cut functions are symmetric submodular, this yields a polynomial-time algorithm for the hypergraph minsum boundary objective at \(k=5\). However, no general polynomial-time algorithm is known for fixed \(k\ge 6\), and no NP-hardness is established for fixed \(k\ge 5\) in the unweighted hypergraph setting.

On the minmax and standard \(k\)-cut sides, polynomial-time solvability for fixed \(k\) is now well developed: minmax symmetric-submodular partitioning admits an \(n^{O(k^2)}\)-time algorithm, and hypergraph minimum \(k\)-cut admits deterministic enumeration algorithms for fixed \(k\). These results supply techniques (terminal-cut enumeration, contraction/uncrossing structure) that may inform future attacks on the minsum boundary objective, but current literature still treats the fixed-\(k\) minsum symmetric-submodular/hypergraph partition problem beyond \(k=5\) as open.

sources.title: Min–max partitioning of hypergraphs and symmetric submodular functions
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper proves that Minmax-SymSubmod-\(k\)-Part is solvable in polynomial time for any fixed \(k\): given a symmetric submodular function \(f:2^V\to\mathbb R\) via an evaluation oracle, one can find a \(k\)-partition \((V_1,\dots,V_k)\) minimizing \(\max_i f(V_i)\) in time \(n^{O(k^2)}T\), where \(n=|V|\) and \(T\) is the oracle evaluation time (Theorems 1.2 and 3.1). As a corollary, Minmax-Hypergraph-\(k\)-Part (minimize \(\max_i w(\delta(V_i))\)) has an \(n^{O(k^2)}m\)-time algorithm for fixed \(k\) (Theorem 1.1).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for the complexity of the *minsum* hypergraph \(k\)-partition objective \(\min \sum_{i=1}^k |\delta(V_i)|\) for fixed \(k\ge 5\). This paper primarily resolves the *minmax* variant (minimize \(\max_i |\delta(V_i)|\)) for fixed \(k\) via a structural theorem (Theorem 1.3) and a contraction-based enumeration algorithm. It does not give a polynomial-time algorithm or NP-hardness for the minsum objective on hypergraphs. It does, however, discuss the minsum symmetric-submodular partition problem (Sym-Submod-\(k\)-Part) and proves a structural theorem for minsum (Theorem 4.1) that yields solvability for Sym-Submod-\(k\)-Part up to \(k\le 5\) assuming an algorithm for Submod-\((k-1)\)-Part; but it explicitly states that a general fixed-\(k\) polynomial-time algorithm for the minsum symmetric-submodular/hypergraph partition problem remains open.

sources.title: A polynomial time algorithm for finding a minimum 4-partition of a submodular function
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper gives the first polynomial-time exact algorithm for the minimum 4-partition problem for a general submodular function: given submodular 
\(f:2^V\to\mathbb R\), it computes a 4-partition \(\{V_1,V_2,V_3,V_4\}\) minimizing \(\sum_{i=1}^4 f(V_i)\) in time \(O(n^6\tau(n))\), where \(n=|V|\) and \(\tau(n)\) is the time for submodular function minimization (Theorem 6.1). As a corollary, it computes a minimum 5-partition for symmetric submodular functions in time \(O(n^{14}\tau(n))\) (Corollary 6.2).
sources.connection: For a hypergraph \(G=(V,E)\), the boundary function \(d(U)=|\delta(U)|\) is a symmetric submodular function. The open problem asks for the complexity of minimizing \(\sum_{i=1}^k d(V_i)\) over \(k\)-partitions for fixed \(k\ge 5\) (unweighted hypergraphs). This paper advances the general submodular-partition landscape by settling \(k=4\) in polynomial time and giving a polynomial-time algorithm for \(k=5\) only in the symmetric-submodular setting via reduction to the (general) 4-partition algorithm. However, it does not provide a polynomial-time algorithm (nor NP-hardness) for fixed \(k\ge 5\) for hypergraph boundary objectives; it explicitly states the fixed-\(k\) submodular \(k\)-partition problem remains open for \(k\ge 5\), and even shows a compatibility-based approach fails for 5-partitions (Section 7).

sources.title: Minimum Cut and Minimum k-Cut in Hypergraphs via Branching Contractions
sources.theorem_or_result: From the title/abstract only: the paper gives algorithms for minimum cut and minimum \(k\)-cut in hypergraphs using a “branching contractions” framework, with running time expressed in terms of \(m\) hyperedges, \(n\) vertices, and \(p\) total hyperedge size. (No precise theorem statement is available from the provided abstract snippet.)
sources.connection: The open problem asks for the complexity of minimizing \(\sum_{i=1}^k |\delta(V_i)|\) over \(k\)-partitions for fixed \(k\ge 5\). This objective is not the standard minimum \(k\)-cut objective (which typically minimizes the number/weight of hyperedges crossing between different parts, counted once per hyperedge). However, algorithms/techniques for hypergraph minimum cut and minimum \(k\)-cut may be relevant as potential subroutines or as a starting point for reductions/complexity comparisons. Based only on the title/abstract, there is no indication the paper studies the “sum of boundary sizes” objective or resolves its complexity for fixed \(k\ge 5\).

sources.title: Deterministic enumeration of all minimum cut-sets and k-cut-sets in hypergraphs for fixed k
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper proves that for every fixed integer \(k\ge 2\), all minimum \(k\)-cut-sets in a hypergraph \(G=(V,E)\) with nonnegative hyperedge costs can be enumerated in deterministic polynomial time. Concretely (Theorem 1.1 / Theorem 2.1), it gives a deterministic algorithm that enumerates \(n^{O(k)}\) minimum \((S,T)\)-terminal cuts for all \(|S|,|T|\le 2k-1\), forms a candidate family \(\mathcal F\) of \(k\)-cut-sets, and returns exactly the minimum-cost \(k\)-cut-sets; runtime \(O(n^{4k-2})T(n,p)+O(n^{4k^2-2k}p)\), where \(T(n,p)\) is the time for a minimum terminal cut in an \(n\)-vertex hypergraph of size \(p\).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for the complexity of minimizing \(\sum_{i=1}^k |\delta(V_i)|\) over \(k\)-partitions (fixed \(k\ge 5\)). This paper instead studies the standard Hypergraph-\(k\)-Cut objective: minimize the cost of hyperedges crossing the \(k\)-partition, i.e. \(|\delta(V_1,\dots,V_k)|\), equivalently minimum \(k\)-cut-set. It provides deterministic enumeration and structural theorems for minimum \(k\)-cut-sets via small-witness terminal cuts. These techniques may be relevant as tools (terminal-cut based structure/uncrossing) but do not address the different objective \(\sum_i |\delta(V_i)|\), nor prove NP-hardness or a polytime algorithm for that objective.

sources.title: Algorithms for new objectives in graph partitioning and generalizations
sources.theorem_or_result: The thesis studies several graph/hypergraph partitioning objectives. Relevant to the open problem, it defines Hypergraph-k-Partition (minimize \(\sum_{i=1}^k w(\delta(V_i))\)) and focuses mainly on (i) Minmax-Hypergraph-k-Partition and Hypergraph-k-Cut (objective \(w(\delta(V_1,\dots,V_k))\)), and (ii) enumeration algorithms for optimum solutions when \(k\) is fixed. It states deterministic polynomial-time enumeration algorithms for fixed \(k\): Enum-Hypergraph-k-Cut in \(O(n^{16k-25}p)\) time and Enum-MinMax-Hypergraph-k-Partition in \(O(k n^{4k^2-2k+1}p)\) time (as summarized in the introduction).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for the computational complexity (polytime vs NP-hard) of Hypergraph-k-Partition with the minsum boundary objective \(\min \sum_{i=1}^k |\delta(V_i)|\) for fixed \(k\ge 5\) in unweighted hypergraphs. This thesis explicitly defines that same objective as “Hypergraph-k-Partition” (a symmetric submodular k-partition instance), but the provided excerpt does not contain a hardness proof or a polynomial-time exact algorithm for this objective for fixed \(k\ge 5\). Instead, the thesis discusses: (a) exact/approx/FPT results for graph minmax objectives, (b) hardness/approx for \(\ell_p\)-norm multiway cut, and (c) enumeration algorithms for Hypergraph-k-Cut and Minmax-Hypergraph-k-Partition. Thus it provides context and related techniques (submodular/hypergraph partitioning framework, enumeration/structural results), but does not resolve the stated complexity question for the minsum boundary objective.
